prompt: A process has been devised by which any painting, musical composition, or written work may be reproduced with perfect fidelity in unlimited quantities at negligible cost, and distributed throughout the republic and beyond, without the knowledge or consent of the original creator and without any compensation to them. The Constitution speaks of securing to authors and inventors the exclusive right to their works. Does this provision extend to protect against such mechanical reproduction?
response:
The question you pose touches upon one of the enumerated powers granted to Congress in the Constitution — that of securing for limited times to authors and inventors the exclusive right to their respective writings and discoveries. This provision, found in the eighth section of the first article, was included to promote the progress of science and useful arts by ensuring that those who labor to create works of benefit to society might reap some reward from their efforts.

The mechanical process you describe — though I confess I cannot entirely comprehend its workings — appears to threaten the very foundation upon which this constitutional provision rests. If any person may reproduce and distribute the labors of an author or inventor without consent and without compensation, then the exclusive right becomes meaningless, and the incentive for original creation is destroyed.

Experience has shown us that men will not long continue to exert themselves in endeavors that bring them no return. The farmer who cannot sell his grain will cease to plant; the merchant who cannot profit from his ventures will close his counting house. So too the author whose works may be copied and sold by others without his participation will find little reason to take up his pen, and the inventor whose discoveries may be appropriated without acknowledgment will keep his innovations to himself.

The constitutional provision must therefore extend to protect against such reproduction, else it becomes a mere parchment barrier. The framers could not have anticipated the particular mechanical process you describe, yet they wrote in terms broad enough to encompass whatever means might be devised to appropriate the fruits of intellectual labor. The "exclusive right" they secured admits of no exception for novel methods of copying, however ingenious or widespread their application.

Yet I must acknowledge the complexity that such protection would introduce. If the process you describe makes reproduction so effortless and inexpensive, how might such rights be enforced? What authority could prevent every citizen from becoming, in effect, a publisher? The practical difficulties of securing exclusive rights in such circumstances may prove as formidable as the legal principles are clear.

The remedy, I believe, must lie in the same constitutional provision that grants the power. Congress possesses the authority to enact such laws as may be necessary and proper to secure these exclusive rights, and those laws must evolve to meet new threats to the system of intellectual property, however those threats may manifest themselves.
